data.title: SDVY: New Strategy, Fresh Outlook For These SMID-Cap Rising Dividend Achievers (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last reviewed the First Trust SMID Cap Rising Dividend Achievers ETF (SDVY) on September 2, 2024, when I issued it a "sell" rating due to its constituents' low expected dividend yield and earnings per share growth rate. Since that review was published, SDVY has delivered a 7.70% return with reinvested dividends, on par with the four other small/mid-cap value funds discussed.

However, what these results don't show is that SDVY bottomed out earlier this year, and that its 15.68% gain from April to October was 2-3x better than DON and REGL. As I'll explain today, these improved returns are the direct result of a significant strategy change that not only nearly doubles the holdings count but also increases portfolio turnover, potentially benefiting shareholders in fast-changing markets. Personally, I like these changes, and in the spirit of giving SDVY another chance, I've upgraded my rating to a "hold," and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

First Trust SMID Cap Rising Dividend Achievers ETF Overview

Index Strategy Update

SDVY tracks the Nasdaq US SMID Cap Rising Dividend Achievers Index, which launched on September 18, 2017, and initially selected 100 securities based on earnings and dividend growth, with reconstitutions once per year. First Trust picked it up shortly thereafter on November 1, 2017, and still markets it as a way for investors to gain exposure to small- and mid-cap stocks "that have a history of raising their dividends and that exhibit the characteristics to potentially continue doing so in the future."

While the stock selection criteria has largely stayed the same, it's crucial to know that the strategy changed in March 2025, and instead of holding 100 stocks and reconstituting annually, the Index now holds four sub-portfolios of 125 stocks in equal weight that reconstitute in alternating quarters. In fast-changing markets, this allows the Index to hold up to 500 stocks if there is no overlap between the sub-portfolios, but practically speaking, it's reasonable to assume something closer to around 200. The current holdings count is 181, which means that 56 additional securities (181-125) qualified that otherwise would not have made it into the Index for another year. The following graphic summarizes the latest criteria for the Index in question (NQDVSMR).

As shown, the Index excludes REITs, including Mortgage REITs, and applies some size and liquidity constraints, such as a float-market-cap requirement of above $1B and at least $2M average daily value traded per security. The Index also requires eligible securities to have a twelve-month dividend rate above their rates from three and five years ago, a cash-to-debt ratio greater than 25%, and a trailing twelve-month payout ratio below 65%. Finally, securities must have earnings per share that are positive and greater than their earnings per share from three years ago.

In my view, the last part is crucial because shareholders should never be holding a portfolio of stocks with essentially zero earnings growth, but that's exactly what I found during my review last September. I believe the Index provider also recognized this problem and opted to dramatically expand the holdings count and "run the numbers" more frequently so shareholders don't get stuck holding stocks whose earnings growth trends have deteriorated. The obvious impact will be substantially greater portfolio turnover, but I expect this change will improve results because the criteria are still solid.

SDVY: Now A Small/Mid-Cap Blend ETF

According to Morningstar, SDVY was still classified in the Small Value category as of September 30, 2025. However, SDVY's Stock Style has changed from Small Value to Small Blend in 2025, and it appears to be more growth-oriented than before, so I'm confident a category change is forthcoming, which also changes its peer group.

Sector Allocations and Top Ten Holdings

As of November 26, 2025, SDVY is highly concentrated, with more than three-quarters of the fund invested in Industrials (32%), Financials (30%), and Consumer Discretionary (16%). These sector allocations aren't very different from one year ago, so while sector diversification hasn't necessarily improved, the 181-stock count helps at the company diversification level.

SDVY's top ten holdings are next, with the fund currently being led by Comfort Systems USA (FIX), InterDigital (IDCC), and EnerSys (ENS). Holding weights range from 0.09-1.63%, which reflects a stock's recent performance and the number of times it ranked inside the top 125 in each sub-portfolio.

First Trust SMID Cap Rising Dividend Achievers ETF Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental statistics for SDVY's top 20 holdings, which comprise 21.76% of the fund on a cash-adjusted basis. As comparators, I've also included summary statistics for the WisdomTree MidCap Dividend ETF (DON), the ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (REGL), the ALPS O'Shares U.S. Small-Cap Quality Dividend ETF (OUSM), and the iShares S&P Mid-Cap 400 Value ETF (IJJ). All these ETFs draw from roughly the same size segment, but have a quick scan of the table below, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

SDVY: A Dividend Growth ETF, Not A Dividend ETF

When I last reviewed SDVY, its Index yield was 2.45%, and after subtracting the fund's 0.59% expense ratio, I estimated shareholders would end up with a dividend yield of 1.86%. Now, with the Index update, SDVY's Index yield is only 1.63%, meaning investors should only expect to net a paltry 1.07%. That declining dividend is going to take another six months or so to get fully reflected in the fund's trailing dividend yield (currently at 1.45%), but you can already see a decline in payments in the last two quarters. SDVY's $0.10 per share payment was its lowest in almost four years, and I expect many shareholders will be disappointed to see a "Rising Dividend Achievers" ETF decrease dividends.

However, the strategy change fully explains this, and even though it could deter new investors, I don't think it should. In fact, the fundamental analysis table also reveals that its current selections are doing exactly what they are supposed to - raising dividends, at an annualized three-year rate of 11.65%, to be exact. That's best in the sample group, and I expect that at this time next year, SDVY shareholders will see solid dividend growth resume.

SDVY: Future Dividend Growth Is Well Covered

For a company to increase dividends without stressing the dividend payout ratio, it must also increase earnings, and that's what I believe is SDVY's biggest fundamental improvement over the last year. Last September, its holdings had only 0.43% estimated earnings per share growth on average, but today, it's the highest in the sample group at 9.57%. Furthermore, its 22.25% dividend payout ratio indicates SDVY's holdings' management teams have lots of wiggle room should market conditions deteriorate. They're also profitable, offering investors 12.40% free cash flow margins compared to 8.33% before, and a 13.75% weighted return on total capital that's about two points better than last September.

SDVY: Relative Valuation Takes An Acceptable Hit

SDVY used to have, by far, the most favorable valuations compared to these peers, but its 15.08x forward P/E and 10.70x trailing P/CF ratios are now the highest in this sample group. However, from a growth-at-a-reasonable-price perspective, it's acceptable. To illustrate, consider the following modified PEG ratios, calculated by dividing an ETF's one-year estimated earnings growth rate into its forward P/E ratio:

  • SDVY: 15.08x / 9.57% = 1.58x
  • DON: 14.14x / 7.15% = 1.98x
  • REGL: 14.66x / 7.35% = 1.99x
  • OUSM: 13.80x / 7.37% = 1.87x
  • IJJ: 15.03x / 7.57% = 1.99x

From this GARP perspective, SDVY offers good value, and just to check, I also made the same calculations using trailing P/E ratios and three-year historical earnings per share growth rates, as follows:

  • SDVY: 15.97x / 12.81% = 1.25x
  • DON: 16.52x / 0.79% = 20.91x
  • REGL: 16.58x / 5.00% = 3.32x
  • OUSM: 14.96x / 4.86% = 3.08x
  • IJJ: 17.63% / 0.77% = 22.90x

I'm pleased that SDVY ranks #1 in this sample group on both versions of the PEG ratio, so while it's undoubtedly losing its valuation edge, I believe the trade-off is worth it.

Investment Recommendation

I have decided to upgrade SDVY to a "hold" and allow it a fresh start, given its updated strategy that results in nearly double the holdings and more timely quarterly updates. I think it's wise to consider earnings growth and payout ratios to support dividend growth and safety, and I was happy to see its current holdings offer an attractive mix of growth, value, quality. To be sure, SDVY still has some drawbacks that likely won't change anytime soon, like its high sector concentration and a 0.59% expense ratio that reduces an already-low Index yield to just 1.07%. However, I think once the strategy is in place for a couple of years, shareholders will see solid dividend growth and will be satisfied to sacrifice some current income for higher total returns. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your comments below.

data.title: QYLG: Gets The Job Done But Underperforms Peers (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLG) is a covered call ETF that provides exposure to constituents of the Nasdaq-100 index, while implementing an option writing strategy to amplify its income levels. When I previously covered QYLG, I issued a buy rating because of its outperformance against its peer fund. As more time passes, I've been able to observe the fund's performance through different market environments. Therefore, I wanted to revisit the fund to assess its performance against some of the more notable peers in the sector.

When looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that QYLG's price has declined by about 9%. Despite this, I commend the fund's ability to recover from its prior lows earlier in the year, which saw the share price decline as much as 30% back in April. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 14.4% over the same time frame. QYLG now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 11.3% and issues payouts on a monthly basis, which makes it an enticing choice for investors seeking income generation within their portfolio.

While the fund gets the job done as a straightforward buy-and-hold income position, there are some clear limitations to QYLG's strategy when compared to some notable peers out there. Despite the fund providing exposure to some of the highest quality growth companies in the world, QYLG's option-writing strategy limits the overall growth potential of the fund. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that QYLG implements to generate the amplified income levels it needs to support distributions.

Fund Strategy

According to the recent fact sheet, QYLG has total assets under management of $123.4M that is spread across 103 different holdings. The fund has a reasonable expense ratio of 0.35%, which may be worth it considering the large yield investors are receiving. As implied by the fund's name, QYLG is comprised of constituents of the Nasdaq-100 index. In order to generate amplified income levels needed to support the distributions, QYLG implements a covered call strategy against its holdings. The fund will typically write options against 50% of its portfolio.

Since the fund is geared towards the Nasdaq-100 index, the breakdown by sector looks quite similar. For instance, QYLG is most heavily allocated to Information Technology companies at 56.1% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Communication Services and Consumer Discretionary investments, which account for 14.57% and 13.43% of assets, respectively. Since the fund requires elevated levels of implied volatility to generate large option premiums, it makes sense for QYLG to be heavily allocated to technology stocks.

The downside to the strategy is that the fund implements an ATM (at-the-money) option strategy. ATM options severely cap the upside price growth that can be captured from its underlying holdings. Unlike OTM (out-of-the-money) options, which allow for some price growth to be captured from its holdings. However, it helps this weakness to be offset by the fact that the fund only writes options against half of its assets.

What makes QYLG unique compared to some high-yielding peers is the fact that the fund actually holds common shares of the equities it is writing options against. Looking at the holdings below, we can see that Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest holding at 10.53% of assets. This is followed by other notable growth companies such as Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), and Amazon (AMZN), just to name a few.

Performance Insights & Comparison

Despite QYLG offering a high starting dividend yield, investors shouldn't confuse this with the fund's ability to automatically provide high total returns. Just to demonstrate this, we can refer to the performance chart below. Since inception, QYLG's share price has only increased by about 10.67%. When including all distributions paid to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 87.35% over the same time frame. As we can see, the ATM option strategy severely limits the share price growth over time. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time.

My biggest issue with QYLG is the underperformance against peers. As the demand for income funds continues to grow, there are a ton of compelling peers for investors to choose from. While their underlying asset allocation and strategy to option writing may differ, the premise of generating income from holdings that make up the Nasdaq-100 index remains similar. I believe the most compelling peers are the following:

  • Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD): dividend yield of ~12.6%.
  • Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ): dividend yield of ~9.8%.
  • NEOS NASDAQ-100 High Income ETF (QQQI): dividend yield of ~13.6%.

As we can see below, QYLG is the smallest in terms of assets under management. Conversely, QYLD is the largest out of the group, with assets under management at $7.98B. QQQI has the largest dividend yield out of the peer group, but all of these funds issue payouts on a monthly basis. So from a purely income perspective, QQQI may be the best choice for investors seeking dividends. QYLD is also managed by Global X Funds, with the main difference being that QYLD writes options against 100% of its assets.

GPIQ also stands out from the peer group due to the fact that its option strategy is dynamic and management adjusts the frequency of options written based on market conditions. So GPIQ may write fewer options during bull markets but may write more options during periods of greater market volatility. I recently published an article on GPIQ if you're interested in learning more.

When running a performance comparison between the peer group, we can see that GPIQ has the highest total return at 40.84%. In comparison, QYLG's lower total return of 33% may be attributed to the fact that its option writing is less flexible than GPIQ's approach. So when markets are rallying, QYLG is still writing options against 50% of its portfolio, which limits growth. When you combine this with the fact that QYLG utilizes an ATM option writing strategy, I anticipate this underperformance to continue over a longer holding period.

Dividend

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.2763 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.3%. The first thing you may notice from the history below is the large spikes in the payout chart. These have historically occurred at the end of the year and are periods where the fund is paying out any excess short-term capital gains they've captured. Unfortunately, the last large supplemental payout was declared at the very end of December with no warning, so it's too soon to know whether or not the fund will issue another supplemental at the end of this year.

If we ignore these large year-end supplementals, QYLG has actually done a great job at keeping the normal monthly payouts within a consistent range. Therefore, QYLG is still a solid fund for investors seeking a very stable and consistent source of income generation. My only concern is the fund's ability to sustain this consistent range in an environment where the Nasdaq-100 declines for a prolonged period of time. For instance, QYLG's payout was slightly lower during April of this year, which is when markets experienced a drastic downturn. So in a scenario where markets trend downward for an extended period of time, QYLG's payouts could fall.

On a more positive note, the distributions tend to be paid out in a tax-efficient manner. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 95.60% of the distributions paid YTD were fueled by return of capital. These return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and, therefore, aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating to a hold at this time. Although QYLG certainly gets the job done in terms of providing diverse exposure to high quality companies and an elevated income level from its assets, the underperformance against peers significantly reduces the appeal. So while I see no reason for existing shareholders to sell out of their position, I believe that investors looking for entry into the high-yield space would be better off with one of the peer funds.

data.title: Calamos International Growth Strategy Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Summary

  • Strong quarterly performance driven by security selection. The Calamos International Growth Strategy returned 6.72% (gross of fees) due to strong stock selection in information technology and financials.
  • Global markets rallied on trade progress and earnings growth. International equities posted broad-based gains during the quarter, with emerging markets leading (MSCI EM +10.95%), while developed markets in Europe and Asia Pacific also delivered solid returns amid monetary policy easing and fiscal stimulus.
  • Portfolio positioned to capitalize on secular themes and regional opportunities. The strategy emphasizes secular growth companies while increasing exposure to cyclical growth in financials and industrials, with a bullish stance on Europe, selective emerging market opportunities (particularly China and India), and improving prospects in Japan.

Investment Manager Discussion

We believe the case for global and international allocations continues to strengthen, supported by exceptional opportunities around the world.

Over recent months, we’ve seen broad gains and moderate volatility, supported by easing monetary policy and fiscal stimulus in many parts of the world. Rising earnings and corporate profits demonstrate that companies are adapting to change and disruption, whether that’s global tariffs, the evolution of the AI ecosystem, or growth opportunities specific to countries and regions.

As we entered the year, many investors worried that “America First” policies and a move away from globalization would exact a steep toll on overseas markets and economies, but the opposite has proven true. Indeed, the policy agenda coming out of Washington has provided catalysts for economies worldwide to move forward with their own pro-growth initiatives, creating many exciting opportunities for investors. Germany’s defense industry is a prime example.

Year-to-date returns across global markets illustrate the breadth of opportunity that has emerged. Many countries and regions are trading at or near multi-year or all-time highs—including Europe, the United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, Japan, and select emerging markets, across capitalizations. We’ve seen the dollar move lower to sideways across most currency pairs, and valuation and interest rate differentials point to further downside, which in turn would support non-US markets.

As we look forward, we see many reasons for optimism—and continued upside for many areas of global equities. Earnings growth estimates are more positive in more places over the next year. Valuations are also attractive in many overseas markets.

Our favorite secular themes include:

  • Artificial intelligence and automation
  • Mass digitization (cloud/infrastructure, data privacy/security, software and services)
  • Globalization disrupted (supply chain reshoring, local infrastructure investment, defense spending)
  • Mobility and connectivity (information, entertainment, commerce anywhere/anytime; IoT)
  • Global demographic shifts (shifts in consumption preferences, lifestyles, healthcare)

Nick Niziolek, CFA

Co-CIO and Senior Co-Portfolio Manager

Dennis Cogan, CFA

SVP and Senior Co-Portfolio Manager

Paul Ryndak, CFA

SVP and Associate Portfolio Manager

Kyle Ruge, CFA

VP and Associate Portfolio Manager

Market Environment

Global equities rallied in the third quarter, as investors responded to progress on key global trade agreements, continued earnings growth, and abundant liquidity. The broad-based MSCI ACWI Index returned 7.74% during the quarter. Developed markets returned 7.36%, as measured by the MSCI World Index, and US equities returned 8.12%, as reflected in the S&P 500. Emerging markets also rallied and outperformed in the quarter, with the MSCI Emerging Markets Index returning 10.95%.

Sectors within the MSCI World Index delivered mostly positive returns in the quarter. Technology (+12.27%, USD terms) and communication services (+10.82%) led the index, while consumer staples (-2.46%) and real estate (+2.94%) trailed other sectors for the period.

US equities surged in the quarter, reflecting key global trade announcements, healthy economic growth, and bullish investor sentiment. Corporate earnings continued to be strong in the period, with many S&P 500 companies surpassing earnings estimates and indicating a more upbeat outlook for the full year. In terms of the broader economy, US GDP has been tracking at over 3% growth. However, recent regional PMI data reflected mixed business conditions, including sticky prices and a slower pace of hiring in manufacturing and services. Concerning monetary policy, the Federal Reserve cut interest rates a quarter point in September. Chair Powell’s recent public comments highlighted both sides of the Fed mandate and struck a balanced tone.

European equities returned 3.66% in the quarter, as reflected in the MSCI Europe Index (USD terms, 3.93% in local currency). We continued to analyze the major initiatives across the region, including critical trade agreements, increased fiscal stimulus, and broader consensus on military spending.

European activity has been supported by higher domestic demand, driven by optimism around fiscal initiatives and better visibility on the outcome of trade negotiations. From a monetary perspective, the ECB held interest rates unchanged at its last two meetings, effectively marking the end of its easing cycle of eight interest rate cuts over one year. Recent minutes showed policymakers maintained a “wait-and- see” approach on inflation. In terms of country performance, Spain and the Netherlands led other markets, returning 12.96% and 9.69% in USD terms, while Denmark and Germany lagged the most, returning -12.96% and -1.12%, respectively.

Developed Asia Pacific also saw robust returns during the quarter (in USD terms), as the MSCI Pacific index returned 7.22% (in USD, 8.58% in local currency). Japanese equities delivered strong gains in the quarter. From a macro perspective, business and consumer confidence surveys showed a pickup in recent months. Australian shares also rose in the quarter, even as the region navigated mixed economic data and ongoing concerns regarding the impact of tariffs. The Reserve Bank held rates unchanged at its September meeting, following a quarter-point cut in August. Hong Kong equities also rallied during the period and added to the region’s strong returns year-to-date. Hong Kong saw better economic activity, with private sector PMI hovering near a six-month high and retail sales notching the highest mark since 2023. Regarding select country performance, Japan returned 8.18%, while Australia and Hong Kong returned 3.47% and 9.08%, respectively (in USD terms).

In the third quarter, emerging markets generated strong gains, with the MSCI Emerging Markets Index returning 10.95% (in USD terms, 12.49% in local currency). Chinese equities rose over 20% in the quarter, adding to gains of over 40% year-to-date, as captured by the MSCI China Index. China shares benefited from continued innovation among technology leaders and clearer signals of a potential trade agreement with the US. Indian stocks declined in the third quarter. Despite the setback, India’s economy generated strong growth, with PMI data showing expansion across services and manufacturing, although trade policy uncertainty continued to affect sentiment. Among individual EM countries, the leaders in the period included China (+20.76%) and South Africa (+20.57%) in USD terms, while the laggards included the Philippines (-7.66%) and India (-6.63%).

Currency. The DXY Dollar Index rose 0.99% in the third quarter, reversing a small portion of its decline year-to-date. The Swiss franc (-0.40%), euro (-0.42%), British pound (-2.11%), and the Japanese yen (-2.63%) each depreciated versus the dollar in the period.

Performance Review

The Calamos International Growth Strategy generated a 6.72% (gross of fees) return and 6.48% (net of fees) return versus a 7.03% gain for the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index. Security selection added value to returns as many of our holdings aligned with our key secular themes and performed well owing to their higher growth fundamentals.

Positive Influences on Performance

Information Technology. The portfolio’s favorable security selection and an average overweight stance in information technology promoted relative returns. Semiconductor materials & equipment and systems software holdings notably helped.

Financials. Security selection and an average underweight allocation in financials added to results, especially in the diversified banks and investment banking & brokerage industries.

Negative Influences on Performance

Consumer Discretionary. Over the period, security selection and an average underweight stance within the consumer discretionary sector weakened returns, as holdings in broadline retail and casinos & gaming hurt relative performance.

Industrials. Security selection and an average overweight stance within the heavy electrical equipment and industrial conglomerates industries of the industrials sector lagged.

Geographic Performance

The portfolio benefited from leading security selection in Europe where the Netherlands and Denmark provided contributions. Similarly, the portfolio benefited from leading security selection in Canada.

In contrast, security selection in the United States negatively impacted performance. Similarly, security selection in Emerging Latin America, specifically those in Brazil and Mexico, underperformed.

Positioning and Portfolio Changes

The portfolio’s regional and country positioning reflects combined inputs from our top-down global framework and our bottom-up security analysis. Our investment team evaluates macroeconomic factors and growth opportunities and actively integrates them into the investment decision-making process. As a wider set of opportunities emerges across regions, our team is:

Emphasizing companies across a mix of cohorts. Secular growth names continue to provide the foundation of our portfolios. We are also increasing participation in cyclical growth, particularly within financials and industrials and boosting commodities exposure. Our positioning in defensive names is more selective; the opportunities are less attractive.

Bullish on Europe and other overseas developed markets. Lower inflation and moderate growth supported by fiscal stimulus provide a favorable environment, including in Germany, the UK, Italy, Spain, the Netherlands and Canada. We’re investing in leaders in technology infrastructure and ecommerce, financials, defense & aerospace, and specialized industrials.

Finding many opportunities in emerging markets. Central bank easing, a weaker dollar, and reduced policy uncertainty create a better backdrop for many emerging markets, including China, India, Brazil, Mexico and South Korea. Key themes in our emerging market positions include the AI supply chain, evolving trade relationships (e.g., friendshoring and reshoring beneficiaries), and EM-consumer plays.

Encouraged by improvements in Japan. These include a return to higher nominal growth, improved corporate governance, and capital efficiency. We’re finding particularly compelling growth potential in industrials and AI infrastructure, semiconductors and equipment, ecommerce, and media.

Outlook

International equities continue to face a range of crosscurrents. We analyze multiple market drivers, including global growth dynamics, fiscal and monetary policy, and geopolitical considerations. With the US administration challenging the status quo globally and continually evolving secular growth tailwinds, we expect periods during which we can capitalize on disruption. Within this environment, we continue to identify a variety of opportunities at the thematic, regional, and market-cap levels.

Regarding portfolio positioning, we are seeing a range of secular growth, cyclical growth, select commodities exposure, and positions with more defensive characteristics. We invest in companies with alignment with our key secular themes, attractive earnings growth, and competitive advantages. From a sector perspective, we see opportunities in technology, industrials, communication services, consumer discretionary, and financials with leading fundamentals. Our active investment approach and long-term perspective position us to take advantage of the volatility and opportunities within international markets.

For additional information, please visit the strategy’s profile page:

https://www.calamos.com/strategies/international-growth/

Returns and Risk/Reward statistics presented reflect the Calamos International Growth Composite, which is an actively managed composite that invests in common stocks issued by companies outside the United States. The Composite was created February 16, 2006, calculated with an inception date of April 1, 2005. The Composite results include all fully discretionary accounts, including those no longer with the Firm.

Foreign (Non-US) Securities Risks: Risks associated with investing in foreign (non-US) securities include fluctuations in the exchange rates of foreign currencies that may affect the US dollar value of a security, the possibility of substantial price volatility as a result of political and economic instability in the foreign country, less public information about issuers of securities, different securities regulation, different accounting, auditing and financial reporting standards and less liquidity than in US markets.

Geographic Risks: From time to time, based on market or economic conditions, certain strategies could invest a significant portion of its assets in one country or geographic region. If a strategy does so, there is a greater risk that economic, political, social and environmental conditions in that particular country or geographic region will have a significant impact on performance and performance will be more volatile than the performance of more geographically diversified accounts. The economies and financial markets of certain regions can be highly interdependent and could decline all at the same time. In addition, certain areas are prone to natural disasters such as earthquakes, volcanoes, droughts or tsunamis and are economically sensitive to environmental events. Alternatively, the lack of exposure to one or more countries or geographic regions could adversely affect performance.

Growth Investing Risks: Growth companies are generally more susceptible than established companies to market events and sharp declines in value. Additionally, growth stocks typically lack the dividend yield that can cushion stock prices in market downturns.

Equity Securities Risk: The securities markets are volatile, and the market prices of the securities may decline generally. The price of equity securities fluctuates based on changes in a company’s financial condition and overall market and economic conditions. If the market prices of the securities owned fall, the value of your investment will decline.

The MSCI Europe Index is a free float-adjusted market capitalization weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of the developed markets in Europe. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index is a free-float-adjusted market capitalization index that is designed to measure equity-market performance in the global emerging markets. The MSCI Japan Index is a free float-adjusted market capitalization weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of the developed markets in Japan. The MSCI Pacific Index captures large and mid-cap representation across five Developed Markets countries in the Pacific region. data.title: CGGR: Growth Exposure With A Balanced Sector Mix
data.description:
The Capital Group Growth ETF (CGGR) is among the options covering the growth investment theme. With an active management style, it is a fund that may suit investors concerned about expensive valuations in the category, thanks to a more balanced sector mix, which has been the norm for this fund, despite an increased allocation to technology relative to last year. The next sections will break down CGGR’s portfolio its core holdings and compare sector exposure, valuation, performance, and tradability characteristics with the S&P 500 and other growth ETFs.

ETF Description & Highlights

CGGR follows a growth-oriented approach, investing primarily in U.S. companies, but with the flexibility to allocate up to a quarter of the capital internationally. The investment methodology is based on research and a stock-picking process run by portfolio managers in charge of each segment of the equity market, targeting companies trading at reasonable valuations and with long-term potential upside, according to the managers’ assessment.

According to the latest figures available on October 31, 2025, CGGR had $17.3 billion in assets and holds a relatively concentrated portfolio of 103 companies. The fund skews toward the higher end of the market-cap spectrum, reflected in an average market cap of roughly $382 billion. This highlights a significant increase from the fund’s $250 billion level back in July 2024, at the time of my previous article covering the fund.

Looking at the size breakdown, the fund’s exposure is led by mega caps at 50.3%, up from 45.3% in July 2024, followed by large caps at 25.3% and mid caps at 19.8%. Compared with the S&P 500, CGGR now carries slightly more mega-cap exposure (50.3% vs. 45.7%) and a lighter large-cap weight (25.3% vs. 34.7%), driven by its recent rotation toward mega caps since July 2024.

The fund’s top ten positions are mostly the same as the market cap-weighted equity indexes. The largest weights include Meta (7.56%) and Tesla (6.36%), both overweighted relative to the S&P 500. Technology names are a substantial part of the top ten list, with Nvidia (5.91%), Broadcom (5.24%), Microsoft (4.70%), and Shopify (2.49%). Completing the group are Alphabet classes A and C (3.14% and 2.57%), Netflix (2.83%), and Amazon (2.36%), key players in the digital economy.

The following table includes a list of growth ETFs that will serve as the peer group for CGGR in this analysis. The first is the Vanguard Growth ETF (VUG), which follows a market cap-weighted index. Next is the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG), which tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Growth Index, also with a market cap-weighted structure. The third peer, the SPDR S&P 500 Growth ETF (SPYG), targets a growth-oriented portion of the S&P 500 and mirrors its standard market cap weighting.

The next set of the peer group comprises funds that incorporate quality into the stock selection process, adding a new angle in the analysis, given CGGR’s fundamentally driven approach. The iShares MSCI USA Quality GARP ETF (GARP) screens for companies that combine growth potential with reasonable valuations and healthy profitability metrics. The next fund, the American Century U.S. Quality Growth ETF (QGRO), blends growth and strong fundamentals in the stock-picking process. Rounding out the peer group is the WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth ETF (QGRW), which scores and selects companies based on quality and growth factors within a systematic methodology.

Technology is CGGR’s largest sector, accounting for 35.2% of the total assets. This is followed by communication services at 18.6% and consumer discretionary at 15.2%, giving the portfolio a clear growth-oriented tilt. The next cluster includes healthcare at 11.3%, industrials at 8.6%, and financial services at 5.8%, which contribute to a slightly more balanced profile. Smaller exposures include energy (1.4%), basic materials (1.3%), consumer staples (1.3%), and utilities (0.9%), all of which are in the low-single-digit range.

It is interesting to note that the fund has increased its exposure to growth sectors, such as technology, from 28.7% to 35.2%, and consumer discretionary, from 11.0% to 15.2%. As a result, CGGR now has overweight positions in communication services (+8.0%) and consumer discretionary (+5.0%) relative to the S&P 500, while remaining essentially neutral in technology (-0.2%) but underweight in financial services (-7.1%) and consumer staples (-3.7%).

If we break down CGGR’s allocation by its largest sectors, we see that in technology, there is a heavy concentration in semiconductors and software. The largest holdings are NVIDIA and Microsoft, though they are underweight compared to the S&P 500. However, it holds core overweight positions in these two segments, such as Broadcom, Micron Technology, and KLA in semiconductors, and Salesforce, the chip-design software provider Synopsys, Adobe, and Fair Isaac, an analytics and credit-scoring company, which is a new position for the fund. Other holdings outside these areas include a relatively modest stake in Apple, the cloud-security provider Cloudflare, and the app-monetization platform AppLovin.

The communication services allocation is also concentrated with only seven names. Meta Platforms is by far the largest position, while Alphabet and Netflix remain very representative. Smaller positions include Roblox, the online-gaming and virtual-world platform; Live Nation, the live-events and ticketing operator; and the cable and broadband provider Charter Communications.

In consumer discretionary, the increase in the fund’s sector exposure comes from larger weights in Tesla and Amazon, the largest positions. The exposure to automakers is complemented by shares of Rivian Automotive, the EV truck and SUV maker, while Wayfair adds exposure to home-furnishings retailers. The restaurant & leisure sub-sector is represented by cruise operators Royal Caribbean and Norwegian Cruise Line, alongside DoorDash, the food-delivery and logistics platform, and Chipotle Mexican Grill. Meanwhile, the housing segment is relatively small with holdings in D.R. Horton.

Outside the big three growth areas, the portfolio still keeps meaningful exposure to other parts of the economy, but employs a selective approach. Core holdings in healthcare include Eli Lilly, Intuitive Surgical, and Alnylam Pharmaceuticals. In industrials, aerospace & defense is the largest segment via TransDigm Group, the aircraft-components and aftermarket provider, GE Aerospace, and FTAI Aviation, while in financial services, the fund leans into consumer finance with holdings in Visa and Mastercard.

CGGR’s growth tilt is straightforward, given its overweight allocation to consumer discretionary (+3.2%) and communication services (+3.2%), even compared to growth peers. On the flip side, its neutral exposure to technology (35.2%) contrasts with all pure-growth peers (VUG, SCHG, and SPYG) and also peers with a further quality tilt, such as GARP and QGRW, as all these have a sector exposure in the 42%-53% range. The exception is QGRO, which also has a neutral 36.25 allocation. While CGGR also overweights healthcare and industrials, there is a high sector correlation of over 0.95 with the peer group. However, this is a bit stronger with quality-growth QGRO and QGRW, due to their more balanced approach. Overall, despite some allocation gaps, the sector consensus among peers remains fairly high and is a key particularity if we compare growth funds with other categories, such as value and dividend funds, where there is a much bigger sector allocation dispersion among peers.

Moderate Valuation Compared To High-Growth Peers

At about 25.7x P/E, CGGR trades at a roughly 14% premium to the S&P 500’s 22.5x P/E. A big part of that premium comes from the fund’s overweight position in communication services and consumer discretionary, sectors that trade at a premium to the S&P 500, while a modest exposure to a value sector like financial services also pushes valuations higher. Another factor comes from higher multiples in technology (47.4x vs. 40.6x P/E), driven by overweight holdings in names like Cloudflare (254.5x), Broadcom (50.0x), and Fair Isaac (52.4x).

CGGR’s higher-growth profile is very clear when compared to the S&P 500. Its historical earnings growth of 17.8% outpaces the S&P 500’s 10.4%, and its sales growth (11.5%) also sits comfortably above the benchmark (8.1%). Much of this comes from sectors where the fund either shows faster earnings growth or carries a meaningful overweight allocation. Communication services is a good example: the fund’s portfolio exhibits slightly better 3-year and forward earnings growth metrics than in the S&P 500, and its overweight allocation in the sector amplifies the impact. Technology also contributes positively, as CGGR’s holdings in the sector have a higher growth profile than the benchmark. At the stock level, overweight positions in companies like Tesla, Meta Platforms, Netflix, Cloudflare, and GE Aerospace all contribute to the fund’s composite earnings growth, more than compensating for the negative effect of the fund’s relative underweights in NVIDIA, Microsoft, and Amazon. Altogether, both the sector mix and the individual stock picking help explain CGGR’s higher growth profile compared to the S&P 500.

On the other hand, profitability indicators are somewhat mixed. The fund’s EBITDA margin of 41.9% is about 6.4 percentage points higher than the S&P 500’s 35.5%, helped by strong margins in technology, financial services, energy, and industrials. Technology remains the key driver, as core holdings such as Broadcom, Adobe, Micron Technology, and KLA exhibit very high EBITDA margins, while names like Meta Platforms and Visa also contribute to the fund’s high profitability.

Meanwhile, CGGR’s ROE of roughly 29.4% is well below the S&P 500’s 45.5%, mainly due to weaker ROE in sectors such as healthcare, technology, and communication services. For instance, several holdings, including Alnylam Pharmaceuticals, Insmed, Cloudflare, and Roblox, show negative or subdued ROE, pulling down the composite ROE, despite holdings in companies with better operational metrics, such as Eli Lilly, FTAI Aviation, Motorola Solutions, Visa, and Netflix. In sum, holdings in early-stage biotech and internet names are a drag for the fund in terms of equity returns, underscoring the case of companies exhibiting the classic combination of strong growth but still weak profitability.

CGGR trades at the lower end of the peer group valuation range, only above QGRO, which is the fund with the lowest valuation in the group. QGRO’s 24.0x reflects a below-average exposure to technology (36.2%), while its heavy allocation to small and mid caps, which combined sit at nearly 40%, helps keep valuations at reasonable levels. In contrast, typical growth funds (VUG, SCHG, and SPYG) and the quality growth QGRW trade at nearly 30x P/E, driven by their more than 60% allocation to mega caps, in addition to an over 40% stance in technology. That said, CGGR and GARP also trade at reasonable levels, which is consistent with their more measured exposure to mega caps. Overall, in terms of valuation, CGGR, GARP, and QGRO are positioned in the middle ground between the S&P 500 and pure-play high-growth funds. However, as is usually the case, this is reflected in their growth profile. Their historical earnings growth is nearly 17% on average, while VUG, SCHG, and SPYG all have delivered earnings growth above 20% over the past years.

Outpacing The S&P 500, Lagging Top-Performing Growth Funds

While CGGR is a relatively new fund, with inception in 2022, its performance so far has followed the norm for growth funds, outpacing the S&P 500’s 20.3% total return over the past three years, with gains of 27.5% in the period, despite a muted performance year-to-date, when it has performed in line with the index. But if we analyze CGGR relative to peers, the comparison gets more complex. The fund has performed in the middle of the range, surpassing SPYG and QGRO but lagging behind more aggressive peers like VUG, SCHG, and GARP in the 3-year window, while delivering the second-worst return in the 1-year time frame, staying only ahead of QGRO.

The fund’s volatility has been high even by growth-fund standards. Its 3-year beta of 1.26 is the highest one in the peer group. This high volatility profile is confirmed by its upside and downside capture ratios of 126 and 116, respectively. Peers like SCHG and GARP also exhibit upside capture in the 125-126 range, indicating a similar historical trend to outperform in bullish periods. However, CGGR’s downside capture is by far the highest among peers, suggesting steeper drawdowns in risk-off environments. This is something that is more tied to holdings in high-growth names rather than the sector exposure, since the fund’s allocation to high-beta sectors is relatively balanced, given its neutral allocation to technology and overweight positions only in consumer discretionary.

With an average daily volume of $133.9 million, CGGR’s liquidity is comfortably higher than smaller peers like GARP, QGRO, and QGRW, though still well below the largest growth funds (VUG, SCHG, and SPYG), underscoring a tight 0.01% spread. The fund’s 16% turnover is on the low side compared to peers, only above VUG, indicating moderate portfolio activity. Its 0.39% expense ratio is the highest among peers, especially compared to growth funds that track indexes. The 0.15% dividend yield is lower than the peer-group average, where typical ranges for growth funds stay within 0.20% and 0.50%. Meanwhile, the fund’s tracking error in the 6%-7% range places it in the mid-to-upper end of the category, along with other quality growth funds (GARP, QGRO, and QGRW). And on risk-adjusted returns, CGGR’s Sharpe Ratio of 137 (3-year) trails top-performing growth funds, such as SCHG, VUG, and GARP, while remaining ahead of QGRO, impacted by CGGR’s above-average volatility.

Final Thoughts

With an investment strategy that lies in a selective stock-picking approach, CGGR’s portfolio exhibits a stronger growth profile than the S&P 500. Yet, its relatively balanced sector mix leaves the fund trading at a discount to more aggressive growth peers, though their growth metrics appear softer on a relative basis. This somewhat moderate stance, with neutral exposure to technology, seems to bode well in this current market backdrop still tied to interest rate expectations amid worries about stretched valuations, particularly in those stocks that have surged, fueled by the AI boom.

That said, CGGR’s meaningful allocations to growth stories, like Meta Platforms, Tesla, and NVIDIA, should continue to add value and support a strong participation in the potential stock market upside. But the fund's high-beta profile is a reminder of the downside risks, especially if growth stocks fall out of favor at some point. Despite that, I think CGGR can still play a role for investors looking for growth exposure, given reasonable valuations and a sector mix less reliant on technology, while its high volatility profile may be mitigated if the position in the fund is part of a diversified portfolio.

data.title: Wasatch Global Select Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

In the third quarter, global equities were up sharply. The U.S. Federal Reserve's interest rate cut helped buoy stocks, as did economic data showing that, while there may be some pockets of concern, the U.S. economy remained steady. Outside the U.S., equities were buoyed by monetary easing from a few key central banks. In this environment, the MSCI AC (All Country) World Index was up 7.62%. The Wasatch Global Select Fund—Investor Class (WAGSX) underperformed the benchmark and ended the period down -4.59%.

The quarter was indeed challenging for the Fund. As such we wanted to provide additional context on recent performance. Much of the Fund's underperformance comes down to a few issues.

First, we owned some stocks that fell during the period. Those holdings had a large effect on results due to the Fund's concentrated nature. A few of our holdings reported slower revenue growth and their stocks were down substantially. For many of those companies, investors seemed to be debating whether the slowdown was due to the macroeconomic environment or to competitive pressures.

We've worked through that same debate. One of the ways we monitor whether the growth potential and quality of a company is still intact is by creating two or three "critical questions" designed to capture long-term insights about the business that if we knew the answer today would cause us to significantly change our position. These questions help us focus our research on where we think the company could be in five or 10 years.

We routinely revisit those critical questions on each company and have done so again. While we sold a couple of stocks, we believe the long-term investment thesis remains intact for most of our detractors in the quarter.

A Low-Quality Rally Has Persisted in the U.S.

Much of the Fund's underperformance relative to the benchmark came from stock selection in the U.S. Within that market, we held some companies that detracted from the Fund's results. The second reason we underperformed is because stocks of low-quality companies have been in favor in U.S. markets. This is a direct headwind for our investment process, which focuses on identifying high-quality, long-duration growth companies. Markets have rallied since President Donald Trump backed away from his initial "Liberation Day" tariffs. And for the most part, the stocks of lower quality companies have been leading the charge.

In our view, many of the top-performing U.S. stocks in recent months have been those of companies that have speculative business models, and often have little to no earnings. They're often tied to popular investment themes such as artificial intelligence, quantum computing, cryptocurrency, nuclear power, aerospace and defense.

The chart below shows how extreme a headwind this has been. The chart displays one way Wasatch's research team assesses whether high- or low-quality stocks are in favor. Through a proprietary process, we screen and rank all the companies in the small- and mid-cap universe based on financial characteristics, such as return on assets and operating profit margins, among other items. Using an aggregate score, we compare how companies in the top quintile perform relative to the bottom quintile. Based on this metric, we've rarely seen a low-quality rally of this magnitude over the past decade.

High vs. Low Quality Performance

Source: Factset. Rolling 25-week performance, beginning September 25, 2015 and ending September 25, 2025, of the top quintile, or "high quality," stocks relative to the performance of the bottom quintile, or "low quality," stocks. The investment universe includes all U.S. companies with a market-capitalization range between $250 million and $10 billion. Quality quintiles are determined based on an aggregate scoring of several financial metrics, such as return on assets and operating profit margins, among other measures. Performance is gross of fees, represents a subset of securities from the defined universe and does not reflect the performance of the Fund. The analysis is presented for illustrative purposes only. The Fund's actual net performance, including fees and expenses, is provided in the standard performance disclosures in the total returns table. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Additionally, a few statistics point to how low quality has been in favor. The statistics are for the Russell 2000® Growth Index, the benchmark for some of our U.S.-focused small cap strategies. For the third quarter, 56 stocks were up 80% or more. Of that group, 82% were unprofitable, and 57% within the group were trading at a multiple of 10 times sales or greater. We view this multiple, which looks at revenue and not earnings, as extreme.

We can't predict when the period favoring the stocks of low-quality companies will end. But periods of low-quality outperformance have historically been short-lived. The stocks of high-quality businesses have outperformed over the long term. We believe that will continue to be the case.

Details of The Quarter

Shift4 Payments, Inc. (FOUR) was one of the largest detractors from Fund performance during the third quarter. A longtime holding in many of our small-cap funds, Shift4 is a U.S. company that provides payment processing solutions for hospitality, retail and e-commerce businesses. Fundamentals for the company remain solid, as organic revenues grew in the high-teens percentages in the most recent quarter. But the stock has been down in part due to concerns about how a softer macroeconomic environment could affect the company. Additionally, investor sentiment on the company became less favorable after its acquisition of Global Blue, which is a foray into a new customer segment for Shift4. The acquisition also means the company is taking on more debt. Acquisitions can sometimes cause investors angst since they take time to integrate into a company's normal operations. However, we have confidence in Shift4's leadership and their ability to execute on Global Blue.

MonotaRO Co. Ltd. (OTCPK:MONOY) , a Japan-based industrial distributor, also detracted. Monthly sales were weak in August, which weighed on the stock. But we think the lighter revenue was due to one-time factors, such as customers that pulled forward purchases of some products in prior quarters. While results were a little disappointing, we have a lot of conviction in MonotaRO. We believe the company provides outstanding service and has advantageous economies of scale compared to other distributors in Japan, which should allow it to take even more market share. Further, we like the company's strategy to grow its business among several client segments.

Another detractor was HealthEquity, Inc. (HQY) , the largest U.S. non-bank custodian for health savings accounts (HSAs). The stock was likely down due to expectations of interest rate cuts. Lower interest rates cause the company to earn less income from money held on deposit for customers. However, we've never held the stock based on a view about interest rates. We actually think investors don't appreciate the steps management has taken to make the company's portfolio less sensitive to interest rate changes. We hold HealthEquity because we think the company is primed for strong growth as more employers continue to offer HSAs. And while the stock was down for the three-month period, we were pleased to see it gain back some ground in September after the company reported quarterly earnings and revenue growth that topped expectations.

One of the top contributors to Fund performance was BayCurrent, Inc. (OTCPK:BYCRF) , a Japan-based IT consultant. BayCurrent's stock was up on the back of strong earnings results. In July, the company reported that it notched quarterly revenue growth of 25.7%, and earnings growth of 29.3% from the same period a year ago. We've mentioned BayCurrent in many previous commentaries, and there has been no change to our long-term thesis. We expect BayCurrent to be a prime beneficiary as companies across Japan look to better digitalize their businesses.

Trip.com Group Ltd. (TCOM) was another large contributor. The company operates the leading online travel agency in China. Net income in Trip.com's most-recent quarter grew 16% from the same quarter a year ago on stronger travel demand, especially during holiday periods. Profitability also improved as margins rebounded following two quarters of pressure from heightened competition and international expansion. With competitive intensity easing, the recovery in margins has reinforced investor confidence in the company's long-term prospects.

The stock price of Halma plc (OTCPK:HLMAF) also moved higher and contributed to Fund performance. Halma is a U.K.-based industrial distributor that focuses on growing end markets such as the safety, environmental and medical categories. Halma has grown both organically and through an acquisition strategy that we see as having been quite successful in recent years. (Current and future holdings are subject to risk and change.)

Outlook and Positioning

While we were disappointed with the Fund's performance for the third quarter, we aren't deviating from our investment process. During Wasatch's 50-year history we've encountered other periods of underperformance. And while these periods are uncomfortable, we've learned the importance of staying committed to our philosophy and process, as opposed to chasing what's currently "working" in the market. We'll always do our best to be open-minded about new opportunities that could unfold, but we'll stay true to our discipline of finding high-quality, long-duration growth companies.

As we look across the Fund, we remain encouraged by the fundamentals of our companies, and by the high-quality nature of these businesses. Earnings growth for most companies held by the Fund remains strong. This makes us excited about the Fund's return potential over a three- to five-year time horizon.

Thank you for the opportunity to manage your assets.

Sincerely,

Mick Rasmussen, Ken Applegate, Mike Valentine and Stuart Rigby

data.title: Coterra Energy: Unloved And Misunderstood
data.description:
Thesis

Coterra Energy (CTRA) is an oil and gas producer that has structured itself around being flexible. The company has two distinct swaths of acreage that allow the company to pivot its investment focus between oil or gas production.

This is made possible by the company owning significant acreage blocks in both the Permian and Marcellus shale plays. By owning two vastly different plays, the company has the freedom to allocate capital toward the commodity with the most promising outlook.

This is vitally important in today’s market, as the previous strengths in oil prices have largely given way to the expected rise of natural gas. Just one year ago, oil was hovering north of $70/barrel, and natural gas had just recently climbed out of the sub-$3/mcf basement.

Today, the tables have been flipped over. By having a diverse portfolio, CTRA can toggle investment between these commodities to yield the highest net return. As a result, the company’s natural gas production will be carrying the lion’s share of the workload to keep the company producing a double-digit free cash flow yield.

Ironically, one of CTRA’s largest investors, Kimmeridge, sent a letter to CTRA’s board advocating for a new strategy. The activist investor has advocated for ditching the Marcellus assets in favor of a pure Permian oil play.

In my view, this is flawed in multiple ways and also underscores a misunderstanding of the value in a two-pronged approach to oil markets.

The Strategy And What Kimmeridge Is Missing

If you type Coterra into your web browser, the company’s tagline of “Flexible and Built For The Future” will appear. I think this sums up the strategy fairly well.

The flexibility to alternate capital spending between three fundamentally different basins is rather unique in the public E&P space. The company’s options range from the liquid rich Permian, to the mixed Anadarko and are rounded out by the dry gas Marcellus Basin.

Each of these basins has its own strengths and thus allows Coterra to lean on each asset at different times. As shown below, this allows the company to generate rather stable FCF, regardless of which commodity is performing the strongest.

One of the more interesting things to note from this slide is how this agnostic approach positions CTRA for the future. If you are a reader of my work, you probably already know that I believe oil to be weak until mid-2027 at the earliest. That’s not exactly a unique perspective, but having the optionality to do something about it is.

Based on forward strip pricing, CTRA believes the remainder of this decade will be more favorable for gas production than oil. In fact, the crude-to-natural gas price ratio stands at approximately 13:1. This level is clearly in the blue territory, where future investments are more likely to be gas-oriented. With 12 years of Marcellus inventory available, CTRA simply has levers to pull that others don’t.

The Other Side Of The Isle

Oddly, this viewpoint is about 180 degrees from that of activist investor Kimmeridge. The investment group believes that CTRA should split off its natural gas assets in the Marcellus and Anadarko to become a pure Permian producer.

Given CTRA’s position as one of the highest return operators in the Delaware Basin, this notion makes some sense. The company has consistently demonstrated the ability to produce strong returns through efficient development of very large multi-well pads. This, in addition to a significant continuous acreage position in Culberson and Reeves counties, would make the company an enticing investment.

However, I believe this message really misses the big picture of what CTRA can provide. This same proposal would have made significantly more sense in November 2024, when natural gas was frequently below $3/mcf.

Maybe this letter just got stuck in Kimmeridge’s outbox, only to be finally released from the depths of the internet. Otherwise, the timing seems odd. It doesn’t make much sense to me to have the company shed the free cash flow potential of the Marcellus business unit. Particularly so, as we stand at the eve of a structurally advantaged natural gas market thanks to both growing data center and LNG demand.

Even so, the Marcellus unit hasn’t gotten much credit for the job it has done thus far in 2025. In the first quarter, the natural gas segment generated more revenue for Coterra than crude oil. This was also when crude traded well north of $70/barrel, making this accomplishment even more impressive.

This quarter was also the largest FCF-generating quarter, producing $663 million. By comparison, Q2 and Q3 generated $329m and $533m in FCF, respectively. As shown below, natural gas has produced 29% of CTRA's Q3 revenue and 35% year to date.

This is a unique aspect that you won't find in any Permian-based producer. Permian producers typically struggle in their natural gas sales due to extreme Waha prices. These producers often have to give their gas away or even pay to get rid of it when Waha trades negatively. Therefore, I believe it is a mistake to count out natural gas performance for CTRA.

Now that we are roughly two-thirds of the way through the fourth quarter and the dead of winter approaches, the importance of Marcellus natural gas to the Coterra strategy again rings true. Current indications are pointing toward a very robust winter natural gas market. Future contracts have already surpassed pricing seen in the previous winter. This will set up CTRA to generate significant FCF in Q4 and Q1-26.

As a result, CTRA has maintained its FCF guidance for 2025 despite the decline in oil revenue. The company currently expects to generate over $2 billion in FCF this year. This would value CTRA at a 10% FCF yield. I view this as a very solid figure considering the challenges the oil market has faced in 2025.

Valuation

From a valuation standpoint, CTRA trades more like an oil company than a natural gas producer. I purposely picked two pure-play Permian producers and two natural gas producers for comparison. Here we can see that CTRA trades more or less in line with Permian peers but also at a significant discount to pure natural gas producers.

While this valuation does not scream bargain, I believe it is a reasonable level for a company that plans to accelerate its cash returns to shareholders in 2026. The company has a stated debt threshold of 0.5x EBITDA before it directs incremental FCF toward shareholders.

CTRA has already paid down $600 million in debt so far this year, reducing total debt to $3.9 billion. This places the company’s current leverage ratio at 0.8x and within striking distance of its stated goal.

This has allowed the company to reinstate its buyback program. Although modest in size, the company has bought back $47 million YTD. Analysts probed this topic a little deeper during the third-quarter conference call to get a better understanding of how 2026 would develop.

Betty Jiang - Barclays

How do you think about the allocation of your excess free cash flow between debt reduction and buyback going forward? Is there a reason not to think we can get back to that 100% return level into next year?

Shane Young - Executive VP and CFO

When you're at sort of the last bit of the repayment, your feeling is a little different than when you're at the first bit of the repayment and the ability to sort of feather in both some buyback activity and continued deleveraging is just much more palatable at these states.

So as we talked about, we reinitiated the program in last month and expect that we'll continue to be opportunistic, particularly where prices have been over the course of the last 4 to 6 weeks. In terms of the future levels that we get to, look, I would only say, look at our past. And in 2024, we returned roughly 94% of free cash flow through the mix of dividend and buybacks. And the year before that, I think we're around 75% in 2023. And look, that's a place we strive to get back to and think we're well on our way to being there.

These statements are more of a directional guide than one with specific return thresholds. However, it appears that the direction is up for shareholders. How far up is likely to be determined by the severity of the winter season. If current pricing stays intact through the heating season, returns are sure to follow.

Risks

As much as natural gas is a tailwind for CTRA, that same sword cuts in both directions. Natural gas pricing has a tendency to be even more volatile than crude oil. This is partially due to weather driving the lion's share of total demand.

The other factor is how quickly and easily producers respond to these seasonal changes. As shown by the latest EIA forecast, natural gas inventories are now essentially equivalent to the Y-o-Y comparison. This is somewhat impressive considering inventories were nearly 700 BCF lower at the start of the spring building season.

Producers have clearly responded to the improved pricing outlook, allowing the market to recover from the depletion. As a result, we will be entering the 2026 winter with a stronger baseline of production. This presents the opportunity for the industry to get too aggressive in its response to the presumed increase in natural gas demand.

If this were to occur, I believe it is possible to push natural gas markets into an oversupplied condition. Because of this, I think it is appropriate to take a conservative stance when Mother Nature is the biggest customer for natural gas.

Investor Key Takeaways

1. Activist Investor Kimmeridge has proposed for CTRA to divest its Marcellus and Anadarko assets to make a pure Permian play.

2. This is in direct contrast to the company’s strategy to pivot investment between the two commodities. The company values flexibility to allow for the most productive deployment of capital.

3. CTRA is capable of generating a 10% FCF yield and trades at a valuation comparable to pure play Permian peers. It trades significantly lower than that of pure natural gas producers.

4. Natural gas appears positioned to generate the bulk of CTRA’s FCF in 2026, while oil remains muted. Prices heading to prime heating season are very constructive, providing significant upside to CTRA.

5. I choose to be conservative on natural gas plays due to the unpredictability of Mother Nature and her consumption of the fuel. US inventories have recovered from the previous winter. This implies a healthier baseline level of production and a non-zero chance of an oversupplied market.

6. I like CTRA’s flexibility, low leverage, and reasonable dividend. I expect shareholder returns to improve in 2026, assuming a normal winter lies ahead. I continue to rate CTRA as a BUY.
